title: Mycelis hispida
taxonomy: Mycelis hispida is classified within the plant family Rosaceae. It belongs to the genus Mycelis, which is part of the order Rosales. The species M. hispida is closely related to M. muralis and M. odontoloma. Mycelis hispida is often found in mountainous regions, particularly in Europe. This species is known to thrive in areas with well-drained soil and full sun to partial shade conditions.
uses: Mycelis hispida, also known as wood sorrel or creeping wood sorrel, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its leaves and stems are said to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which can help in treating wounds and skin issues. The plant is also believed to have astringent properties, which can aid in reducing bleeding and promoting healing. In some cultures, Mycelis hispida is used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and stomach ulcers, due to its potential antimicrobial effects. However, further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Mycelis hispida for medicinal purposes.

intro: Mycelis hispida, also known as hispid butterbur, is a perennial plant species belonging to the family Compositae, native to Europe and Western Asia. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and anti-allergic properties, providing relief from conditions such as migraines, allergic rhinitis, and asthma. The bioactive constituents of M. hispida include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Herbal preparations made with M. hispida include teas, tinctures, and extracts, often used to alleviate symptoms of migraines and allergic reactions.
